text: The video shows both direct and indirect hernias. On the left side, the sigmoid colon is adhered between the hernias. The direct space and indirect space are visible, with the bladder and the inferior epigastric vessel located between these spaces.

The surgeon identifies the median umbilical ligament and observes a pantaloon hernia on the right side, with visible direct and indirect spaces. The transabdominal preperitoneal approach begins with dissection six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the deep ring at the two o'clock position, followed by the creation of a pre-peritoneal pocket.

The surgeon uses cold scissors to cut the peritoneum, avoiding monopolar energy. Initially, harmonic energy was used, but now the scissors are employed exclusively. The triangle of doom is visible at the level of the deep ring. The peritoneum is stretched medially and inferiorly to separate the inferior epigastric vessels, which are located in this area.

The surgeon continues dissection six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the deep ring at the two o'clock position, progressing towards the medial umbilical ligament. The dissection stops medial to the ligament, and a large 15x30 mesh is prepared for placement, resembling a butterfly shape, without cutting the medial umbilical ligament.
The surgeon performs medial dissection, entering the retropubic space. The bladder is visibly separated downward, allowing access to the space of Retzius.

The surgeon identifies the pubic symphysis and the left side of the Cooper's ligament, continuing lateral dissection to enlarge the pocket for the introduction of a 15 by 30 mesh. The dissection occurs in a clear aerial plane, focusing on peritoneal separation.
The surgeon identifies the triangle of pain, located lateral to the triangle of doom, and enlarges the pre-peritoneal pocket. The spermatic vessel is visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a grasper to pull the hernia sac inferomedially, separating it from the transversalis fascia. The peritoneum is visibly pulled as the sac is maneuvered, allowing for clear separation from the fascia.

The surgeon identifies the level of the ring with the inferior epigastric vessel visible above. Medial dissection is performed in the direct space, separating the transversalis fascia of the direct hernia from the peritoneum. The peritoneum is seen protruding into the defect. The Cooper ligament on the right side is visible, with the bladder located below in the retropubic space.

The surgeon demonstrates the left and right sides of the Cooper ligament, with the hernia sac now extracted from the defect. The sac provides additional peritoneum, which is visible outside the defect. The pocket is further enlarged around the defect area to facilitate mesh placement, ensuring complete coverage.

The surgeon positions the mesh between the peritoneum and surrounding structures, with the spermatic vessel visible laterally and the vas deferens medially at the level of the deep ring. The sigmoid colon is seen above, situated between the direct and indirect hernia spaces, and is mobilized to be dropped down, facilitating the creation of a pocket around the defect.

The surgeon uses scissors in the left hand to start dissection six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the hernia defect at the ten o'clock position on the left side. The dissection begins from medial to lateral, starting at the medial umbilical ligament. The inferior epigastric vessel is visible, and the indirect space lateral to it is being separated to accommodate the mesh.

The surgeon enlarges the pocket to accommodate a larger mesh. The sac is pulled out on the left side and separated from the transversalis fascia. The inferior epigastric vessel is visible, with the direct hernia located medial to it. The peritoneal sac is moved downward while the transversalis fascia is elevated.

The surgeon identifies the muscle defect and separates the hernia sac, revealing extra peritoneum. The Cooper ligament, visible as a pearly white structure, is dissected on the left side, with the bladder located below. The dissection between the medial umbilical ligament and the bladder is nearly complete, and further separation at the level of the ring is performed to enlarge the pocket for a large mesh placement.

A 30 by 15 centimeter mesh, cut in a butterfly shape, is prepared to cover the hernia defect. The mesh is rolled and passed between the medial umbilical ligaments. A grasper is introduced from the right side to pull the mesh from left to right, ensuring the peritoneum remains intact to support the mesh and prevent migration.

The surgeon uses harmonic scissors to cut the vicryl suture and unfolds the large mesh on both sides. The mesh is carefully positioned to cover the entire myopectineal orifice without rotation, ensuring it is spread evenly across the left and right sides. The mesh is not fixed with any tackers, employing a double brass technique.

The surgeon uses the extra peritoneum obtained from the sac removal to reinforce and cover the mesh over the hernia defect. Small buttonholes are visible due to the pantaloon shape of the hernia, but the peritoneum effectively covers these areas. Five tackers are used on either side to secure the peritoneum over the mesh, without directly fixing the mesh itself. The procedure is performed on the left side using the transabdominal preperitoneal technique.
